data.title: Tesco Outperforms On All Fronts
data.description:
One might be forgiven for mistaking Tesco PLC (OTCPK:TSCDF) (OTCPK:TSCDY) for a high-flying tech company rather than a brick-and-mortar grocer. Tesco's share price has grown by a third over the past year; not only outperforming the FTSE 100 index and its closest competitor Sainsbury's by four times, but also outperforming Europe's largest technology company, ASML, by three times, and outperforming Magnificent Seven tech giant Alphabet by a third.

The past few years have been kind to Tesco. But fortune favours the hard-working. True, the inflationary environment of the past three years has served Tesco well by lifting retail prices, and giving Tesco a welcome boost in its sales. But Tesco did not just ride the wave of inflation lazily; a lot of hard work meant it went along.

Record share price is associated with record sales and profitability

Tesco is continuing to uplift its sales and profitability consistently - and impressively. In the last financial year ending February 2024, Tesco achieved sales of GBP 68 billion - 17% higher than five years ago. Six-month sales for the period ending August 2024 were a record of GBP 34.8 billion. Operating income of GBP 1.6 billion in those six months equated to a 4.5% net income margin, which was well above the operating income margin of 4.1% for the full year and for the comparable period ending August 2023. Progress is clear.

Tesco's UK like-to-like food sales grew by a solid 4.9% in the first financial half of 2023/24 while clothing and home items achieved a rare growth rate of 1.6%, excluding toys. Total UK retail sales achieved a healthy overall like-to-like growth rate of 4%. Tesco does not disclose the division between value and volume sales growth, but the company reported that volumes drove growth across both the UK and the international business, driven by successful customer offerings. This is the main distinguishing factor between Tesco and other retailers; managing to grow retail volumes in mature, slow-moving markets.

Not only is Tesco growing sales and profitability nicely, but it is also generating strong underlying cash flows. GBP 2.6 billion of operating cash flows, to be exact, in H1, coupled with GBP 1.3 billion of free cash flows. Low levels of capex for this maturing business, coupled with persistent positive working capital cash contribution - typical for a grocery business - underpin strong cash generation that is likely to remain so in the foreseeable future. This provides support to maintain and grow the attractive 3.5% dividend yield. The interim six-month dividend per share jumped by 10%, demonstrating Tesco's confidence in performance and outlook.

Tesco maintains a stable credit rating of BBB- rating, while improving its debt metrics through the better generation of operating cash flows and keeping debt stable. Net debt to retail operating cash flow should reach 3.4x by the end of the year, a welcome drop from 3.7x a year ago. An asset-heavy company such as Tesco will always need to manage a large volume of debt; either in owning stores, warehouses, and equipment or in leasing them.

In the six months ending August 2024, Tesco expanded its market share by 62 basis points to 27.8% - more than double the expansion rate of a year ago.

Inflation slowdown is reflecting on soft landing in sales growth

While the past three years were bountiful times for retailers, as inflation was lifting sales revenues without much sweat, now retailers have to sweat it out in order to manage growth. Food inflation in the UK is still decent at an annualised 1.8% level. Tesco is doing better than others in combining slower grocery price growth with stronger product offerings, store upgrades, investing in online growth, and investing in customer satisfaction. Sales growth did, as expected, decrease from around 10% a year ago to 4% now, but this was expected, and is still acceptable for behemoth retailers operating in mature developed markets.

The slowdown of inflation will have tailwinds for investors; the large debt that Tesco carries will soon become much cheaper to service, with the Bank of England and other major central banks heading in a one-way direction of decreasing interest rates. Tesco was paying an average of 5% on its debt (a lowly credit rating does not help) but expects that to hover closer to 3.5% in a year's time.

Economic headwinds or tailwinds from the new government?

Whether the new Labour government in the UK is positive or negative for business is anyone's guess. Ahead of the 30th of October Autumn Budget, we will keep guessing. The initial messages from the government about supporting investment and growth have been countered lately by more determined messages about raising taxes and cutting spending. With the UK debt hitting 100% of GDP - the highest since the 1960s - these later messages seem credible.

UK consumers are already licking their wounds after three years of inflation combined with high interest rates - the last thing they need is additional financial pressures. This reflects on the consumer confidence parameters, which show that consumers - understandably - do not have a boost of confidence at the moment.

Management strategy is modest but acceptable for a defensive investment

One might wonder whether Tesco's management could do with a bit of ambition. Their strategy and plans are focused on 'more of the same'; improving customer satisfaction, growing digital and improving productivity and cost management in order to offset lower price inflation. That is all good but lacks any form or shape of excitement. But Tesco never made claims to be the most exciting stock on the block; it is happy to be viewed as a defensive, plain vanilla business. And investors do not seem to mind, given the 50% increase in share price over the past five years.

Valuation is still tempting

Despite reaching record highs, as I write this article, Tesco is still not an overvalued investment. A P/E ratio of almost 13.5x might not seem particularly cheap, but it is lower than 14.5x a year ago. UK peer Sainsbury's is trading at a whopping 48x, and US cousin Walmart is trading at 42x. Tesco does cheap in comparison, but a further discount in price would entice value shoppers even more.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: M&T Bank: Improved Credit Quality Highlights A Strong Q3
data.description:
Shares of M&T Bank Corporation (NYSE:MTB) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining over 50% to a 52-week-high. That rally appears set to continue Thursday, with the stock gaining a further 4% in response to solid quarterly earnings. I last covered MTB in July, rating shares a “hold,” though given their 10% gain vs. the market’s 5% rally, a “buy” would have been justified. I remain a hold.

In the company’s third quarter, M&T earned $4.08, beating estimates by $0.46. This was a strong quarter with expense discipline and impressive net interest performance. Credit trends are particularly heartening, increasing my confidence in the sufficiency of its reserves. Non-interest expenses were up a modest 2%, helping to sustain operating margins. Deposits were the one blemish on the quarter, in my view, but even here, the data is mixed rather than negative. In other words, underlying results support the positive reaction we are seeing in shares today.

Looking first at net interest trends, interest income gained $8 million to $1.74 billion in Q3. This increase was aided by 1 extra calendar day. Additionally, net interest margin (NIM) expanded by 3bps to 3.62%. Last quarter, there was a 7bp gain due to unexpected payments on non-accrual loans, so some moderation was to be expected. Increasing further despite the lapse of this tailwind was a positive.

On the liability side, there was a 2bps benefit from lower funding costs, as it continues to pay down higher-cost wholesale funding. Additionally, interest-bearing deposit costs fell 2bps. During the quarter, the average Fed funds rate fell by ~5bps, implying a 40% flow-through to deposits, (levels above 35% are solid). It is encouraging to see M&T bring down deposit costs alongside short-term interest rates, though the absolute level of deposits remains underwhelming.

As you can see below, M&T had $161.5 billion of average deposits, down $2 billion sequentially. It did grow checking accounts while letting more higher-cost CDs mature, a positive mix shift. Non-interest-bearing (NIB) deposits fell a further $1.5 billion, an acceleration from last quarter’s decline. This is an area where I am hoping to see banks near their floor, and some, like Citizens Financial Group, Inc. (CFG) are showing decelerating outflows. It appears M&T has not yet found the floor to these balances.

Interestingly, while average balances declined by $2 billion, on an end of period basis, deposits grew by $4 billion to $164.6 billion. This was because deposit momentum was weak at the start of the quarter but finished more strongly. It will be important to see this momentum continue. M&T has a stable deposit base, but over time, deposit growth is critical to growing assets and earnings power.

Given lower deposits, there was a 1.2% decline in interest-bearing assets to $191.4 billion. MTB was able to grow NII even with fewer assets because of higher yields on its asset base. It registered 3bps of benefit from fixed reinvestment. M&T has $31 billion of securities, up $1.3 billion. With improved liquidity and capital, it has been able to grow this portfolio again. Thanks to higher short-term yields than on maturing securities, its portfolio yield was up 9bps to 3.7%. It has a 3.6-year average duration, limiting mark-to-market exposure from swings in interest rates.

Average loans were essentially flat at $134.8 billion, but there was a meaningful mix shift here. CRE loans declined by 8% or $2.4 billion, while C&I loans increased by 3% or $1.6 billion; consumer loans also grew. M&T has significantly pulled back underwriting new commercial real estate loans, allowing these balances to decline as it focuses more on business lending.

This effort is delivering results beyond just loan composition; it is helping improve my confidence around loan quality. M&T has $10.9 billion of criticized C&I and CRE loans, down $1.2 billion sequentially. I would note that 96% remain current, but these are loans that have a higher risk of defaulting. Declining balances suggest potential losses in a downturn will be smaller than previously feared by regulators.

This decline is consistent with other metrics of credit quality. M&T took 35bps of charge-offs, down 6bps from Q2. As a consequence, it took $120 million in provisions for credit losses, down $30 million sequentially and from last year. With this level of provisioning, it held reserves flat. As you can see below, non-accrual loans fell for a second straight quarter and are now lower than a year ago. This decline stands in contrast to banks with more consumer and credit card exposure, where quality has continued to soften.

Because non-accruals are falling, but it held reserves flat, coverage is improving. With $2.2 billion of allowances, that gives M&T 114% coverage, which is still a bit light. If delinquencies were to rise again, I would expect MTB to resume building reserves. However, if we see an economic soft landing that keeps delinquencies at similar levels, M&T should be able to maintain a $110-$140 million quarterly provision rate.

Importantly, even if losses tick up a bit, M&T is well capitalized. It has a common equity tier 1 capital ratio (CET1) of 11.5%, up 0.1% sequentially. This enabled $200 million of buybacks, a pace that may increase next year. Its 2.9% dividend yield is also secure.

Forward guidance was reasonable, in my view. It expects a similar level of NII and a similar NIM. I would note that its average deposit range of just $160+ billion, implies flat to lower average balances, as in Q3. Given the EOP build, this is a bit disappointing. At this time, I am hesitant to assume sustained deposit growth until we see it. Still, MTB is improving the profitability of its existing asset base, given fixed reinvestment and loan repositioning. It expects to do another $200 million of buybacks.

Back in July, I felt M&T had $15-$15.50 in forward earnings power. Having progressed 3 months further and seen more encouraging data on credit quality. I now see forward earnings of about $15.50-$16.25. This assumes a flat total asset base and flat deposits at current levels. That gives shares a forward multiple of 12x.

We have seen multiple expansion across the sector over the past few months, given the market rally and lessened recession fears. With strong credit quality, this premium multiple to peers with more CRE exposure is justified. However, I believe to grow earnings more quickly and widen its multiple further, we will need to see deposit growth accelerate, which remains an outstanding question. As such, I remain a “hold” on M&T Bank Corporation stock. It has greatly improved the efficiency of its asset base, lifting shares, but with that theme largely played out, growing its asset base will be critical to extending the rally.

data.title: My 9.5% Income Portfolio-Fifty Shades Of Blue
data.description:
Pontormo and Mannerism

Behind the Ponte Vecchio in Florence, at the beginning of the road leading to the Pitti Palace, there is a small square overlooked by the church of Santa Felicita. We are in the Oltrarno district, the area of the city located on the left bank of the Arno River, enlivened by old stores and small cafes hidden in picturesque alleys in which a genuine Florentine atmosphere can still be breathed.

The church is an integral part of the so-called Vasari Corridor, a sheltered walkway that connects Palazzo Vecchio with Palazzo Pitti via the Uffizi and over the Ponte Vecchio. It is the heart of Medici Florence, inside which is preserved the Deposition by Jacopo Carucci, known as Pontormo (1494-1557), a painter of the Florentine school who pioneered Mannerism.

Mannerism was the artistic current, of refined and nonconformist style, that marked the end of the Renaissance, and Pontormo’s tempera on panel painting (datable to around 1526-28) represents a founding masterpiece of it. His composition lacks any spatial reference: there is no landscape but an aerial space with a profusion of light and evanescent figures that seem to fly, occupying nearly every portion of the surface.

Beyond the composition, however, the charm of this painting lies in its soft, pale, suffused hues. The chiaroscuro is almost absent, and consequently the perception of volumes is reduced, diluted in the reference of colors, among which pinks, blues, greens, and faded yellows prevail. All soft, delicate tones, but so many shades in those light blues!

Let’s Take a Break

Every time I stop to look at this work, as well as others by Pontormo, I wonder how many tones his mind was capable of perceiving to offer such luminous and extraordinarily balanced compositions. All this, while outside the church of Santa Felicita flows the frenetic, bipolar world of today. A world that does not allow nuances between different ideas, opinions and tastes, on the big issues of life as on small condominium disputes.

Even in the field of investments, schools of thought confront and oppose each other, sometimes without being able to find shareable syntheses. As for me, I consider this space that Seeking Alpha grants me as an agorà, the main square of ancient Greek cities, in which to gather to discuss the issues that unite us, first and foremost in how to succeed in making the most of our savings with the instruments made available by the market. In my case CEFs, ETFs, a few BDCs and an ETN. I am not so experienced as to venture out to research individual stocks, so I am “content” with what I can get out of these securities, which in any case is not little.

Over the years, I have gradually developed my own method of selection, based primarily on NAV performance, but to catch my breath I have decided to set aside my convictions on this subject for once and focus today on the market pricing of my beloved funds. Basically, I decided for this article to focus on price, comparing it with the lows and highs touched by each security in my portfolio over its history. I will use this as a way to provide an overview of the path each one has taken from launch to current market quotation and a personal assessment of the possible room for maneuver with respect to those values, while not losing sight of its relationship to NAV. Price is what you pay (according to the mood of the moment,) but NAV always represents what you really buy!

My Overall Portfolio

As you may know, my assets today include 28 securities (19 CEFs, 5 ETFs, 3 BDCs, 1 ETN) organized into three different portfolios:

Cupolone Income Portfolio (named after Brunelleschi’s Florentine dome) consists of sixteen CEFs with monthly distributions:

  • BlackRock Science And Technology Trust (BST)
  • Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income (CCD)
  • Calamos Global Total Return (CGO)
  • Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income II (EOS)
  • Eaton Vance Tax-Adv. Global Dividend Opps (ETO)
  • Eaton Vance Tax-Adv. Dividend Income (EVT)
  • Guggenheim Strategic Opportunities (GOF)
  • John Hancock Tax-Adv. Dividend Income (HTD)
  • Pimco Corporate & Income Strategy (PCN)
  • Pimco Dynamic Income (PDI)
  • John Hancock Premium Dividend (PDT)
  • Pimco Corporate & Income Opportunities (PTY)
  • Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty (RQI)
  • Special Opportunities Fund (SPE)
  • Cohen & Steers Infrastructure (UTF)
  • Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG)
  • XAI Octagon FR & Alt Income Term Trust (XFLT)

Giotto Income Portfolio (named after the fourteenth-century Florentine painter and architect) includes four ETFs and one ETN with monthly distributions that adopt a covered-call strategy:

  • JPMorgan Equity Premium Income (JEPI)
  • JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income (JEPQ)
  • Global X Nasdaq 100 Covered Call (QYLD)
  • Global X Russell 2000 Covered Call (RYLD)
  • Credit Suisse X Links Crude Oil Shares Covered Call ETN (USOI)
  • Global X S&P 500 Covered Call (XYLD)

Masaccio Income Portfolio (named after the founder of Renaissance painting) contains three BDCs and two CEFs with quarterly distributions:

  • Ares Capital Corp (ARCC)
  • Crescent Capital (CCAP)
  • Fidus Investment Corp (FDUS)
  • Barings Corporate Investors (MCI)
  • Royce Value Trust (RVT)

Cupolone Income Portfolio

BST today has a purchase price located roughly midway between the 2016 low and the 2021 highs, after a period low ($28.07) touched during 2022. The long-term trend appears to be upward, with ample room for ascent toward the all-time highs, and the fact that it trades at a discount of -3.37 to NAV makes it in my eyes quite an interesting fund.

CCD has a purchase price located about 25 percent below the highs touched in 2021, after a descent that lasted almost two years and the sharp rebound in recent months. The margins for recovery seem clear even if the very strong premium on NAV (+30.48%) does not make me imagine all that much convenience in buying it at the moment.

CGO has a purchase price located 38 percent below the highs touched in the years following its launch. The fund has shown some vitality over time, with alternating highs and lows characterized by notable recoveries, albeit with a progressively downward trend. The slight premium to NAV (+1%) does not make me imagine much convenience in buying it, even though in recent months it has moved decisively away from the period lows ($7,90) touched a few months ago. A fund on which, however, I am progressively lightening my position, with the intention of closing it out.

EOS today has a purchase price located about 12% below the highs marked in 2021, after a steep descent and a pretty convincing ascent, propelling it back toward those highs. The trend appears to be upward, and its slight discount to NAV (-4.22%) makes it a rather attractive fund in my eyes.

ETO today has a purchase price located 27.5 percent below its all-time highs in the years since its launch. In 2021, the stock marked a period high at $33.80, toward which it is climbing back up after a descent that lasted a couple of years. The trend appears to be upward, and its excellent discount to NAV (-8.88 percent) makes it very attractive in my eyes.

EVT today has a purchase price located roughly 20 percent below its all-time highs in the years since its launch. In 2021, the stock marked a period high at $30.08, toward which it is climbing back up after a descent that lasted a couple of years. The trend appears to be upward, and its excellent discount to NAV (-9.27 percent) makes it a very attractive fund in my eyes.

GOF has a purchase price located about 35% below its all-time highs, touched more than a decade ago, after a more than two-year descent from the period high marked in 2021 ($21.98) and the recent rebound. The very strong premium to NAV (+31.94 percent) does not make me imagine a convenience in buying, although with the rate cut it may have further room for recovery. However, all the previous highs appear to me very distant and difficult to repeat.

HTD today has a purchase price located roughly 17 percent below the all-time highs marked on the eve of the pandemic. After rising between 2021 and 2022, the stock marked a period low in the fall of 2023, from which it began a convincing climb toward all-time highs. The trend appears to be upward, and its discount to NAV (-6.35%) makes it a very attractive fund in my eyes.

PCN has a purchase price located about 25 percent below the high touched on the eve of the pandemic, which was then followed by a second, slightly lower high in the summer of 2021. Since then a two-year descent began, interrupted in recent months by a rebound that brought it to its current values. The strong premium to NAV (+6.20 percent) does not make me imagine all that much convenience in buying, although with the rate cut it may have further room to recover.

PDI has a purchase price located about 27% above the lows touched in recent months, after a descent that lasted more than two years and a recent rebound. However, the pre-pandemic highs appear very distant and difficult to repeat, as well as the last period high, marked in 2021 ($29.60). The strong premium on the NAV (+14.34%) does not make me imagine all that much convenience in buying this fund, although with the rate cut it could have further margins of recovery.

PDT today has a purchase price located approximately 30 percent below the all-time high, marked on the eve of the pandemic. After the descent in 2020 and a strong recovery during 2021, the stock marked a period low in the fall of 2023, from which it started a discrete rebound. The trend appears to be upward, and its slight discount to NAV (-3.98%) makes it fairly attractive in my eyes.

PTY has a purchase price located about 35 percent below the high touched a decade ago. After the recent period high ($21.66) in the summer of 2021, the stock then slipped to a new low ($11.50) in the fall of 2022, from which it started a rebound that brought it to its current values. The strong premium to NAV (+23.72%) does not make me imagine all that much convenience in buying this fund, although with the rate cut it may have further room to recover.

RQI today has a purchase price located roughly 47 percent below its all-time high since its launch. After a disastrous crash at the time of the Great Recession, the stock embarked on a long and steady recovery that led it to a period high at $18.22 in January 2022. The subsequent downturn, which lasted a couple of years, brought it to a low of $9.17 from which a slow recovery began. The trend appears to be upward, and its slight discount to NAV (-1.94 percent) makes it a fairly attractive fund in my eyes.

SPE today has a purchase price located roughly 25 percent below its all-time high, set a decade ago. The outbreak of the pandemic brought it to all-time lows, from which it initiated a very strong rebound followed by a subsequent descent to new period lows ($10.26) about a year ago. Since then a recovery has begun that has brought it to its current levels. The trend appears to be upward, and its very strong discount to NAV (-13.57%) makes it a rather attractive fund in my eyes.

UTF today has a purchase price located approximately 14 percent below its all-time highs, marked in the summer of 2021, after which it started a descent that brought it to its period lows ($18.36) about a year ago. Since then a recovery has begun that has brought it to its current levels. The trend appears to be upward, and despite quoting more or less at par with NAV, it is a very interesting fund in my eyes, given also the proximity to its absolute highs.

UTG today has a purchase price located about 18 percent below the all-time highs marked on the eve of the pandemic. After a new period high ($35.44) in the summer of 2021, the stock then started a descent to a low of $23.52, from which it rebounded vigorously to its current level. The trend appears to be upward, and despite quoting at a slight discount to NAV (-1,41%), it is a very attractive fund in my eyes, apparently projected toward its absolute highs.

XFLT has a purchase price more than 40% below the highs touched at launch in 2017, though far from the 2020 lows. The albeit modest premium on NAV (+2.56 percent) does not make me imagine a convenience in buying it, given also the current sideways phase in a sagging picture since launch and in my eyes with very little hope of reversal. For this reason I decided to progressively lighten my position, with the intention of closing it out.

Giotto Income Portfolio

JEPI today has a purchase price located about 6 percent below the highs marked in January 2022. After a decline that brought it to a period low of $50.85 in the fall of 2022, the fund has started a sharp recovery to current levels. The trend appears to be upward toward absolute highs, making it very attractive in my eyes.

JEPQ today has a purchase price located less about 2 percent below the highs marked just three months ago. After a decline that brought it to a period low of $49.66 in early August, the fund has started a recovery to its current levels, propelling it toward absolute highs and making it extremely attractive in my eyes.

QYLD has a purchase price almost 30 percent below the highs touched at launch in fall 2013, although it has moved away from the lows ($15.36) of fall 2022. The current recovery attempt bodes well, although the successive relative highs over time (which, moreover, have always been declining) still appear far away and difficult to repeat, in a sagging picture since launch.

RYLD has a purchase price more than 36 percent below the highs touched after launch and nearly replicated ($25.67) in the fall of 2022. Since then it has begun a slow and inexorable descent to current values, with an all-time low of $15.18 in early August 2024. The overall picture looks very compromised and, in my eyes, leaves very little hope for a turnaround. For this reason I decided to progressively lighten my position, with the intention of closing it out.

USOI has lost almost 90 percent from its maximum in 2019. There is not much to say about it, except that its presence in my portfolio is tied solely and exclusively to the fact that it is the only security whose “coupons” (as the management house calls them) allow me to offset past losses: let'say I pretend they are “tax free” for me. However, juicy coupons such as the one paid in September ($2.1248) justify in my eyes the maintenance of a modest position in my portfolio.

XYLD has a purchase price about 21 percent below the two all-time highs touched in 2018 and then on the eve of the pandemic. After a new period high at $51.07 in January 2022, the fund has since fallen twice around period lows below $38. The current recovery attempt bodes well, although the three successive highs over time (which, moreover, have been declining) still appear distant and difficult to repeat.

Masaccio Income Portfolio

ARCC has a purchase price about 4.5 percent below the highs touched in January 2022. After a slight decline, the trend appears to be recovering toward absolute highs, making it a very attractive stock in my eyes.

CCAP has a purchase price 11 percent below the highs touched in fall 2021. After a fair downturn, the trend appears to be recovering toward absolute highs, making it a very attractive stock in my eyes.

FDUS has a purchase price about 12 percent below the highs touched a decade ago. After the collapse occurred at the outbreak of the pandemic, the stock bolted a convincing recovery that brought it within sight of those highs. Then, in January 2022, it initiated a sideways phase that brought it to its current values. The trend appears to be upward and makes it very attractive in my eyes. For this reason I plan to increase my position with the money from the funds I decided to liquidate, hoping to purchase it at at a lower price.

MCI today has a purchase price located about 37 percent below the all-time high, which was moreover marked with a spike almost fifteen years ago. Otherwise, the fund’s path has been steadily upward, up to the current level that represents the highest point since the aforementioned all-time high. Despite the high premium to NAV (+17.18 percent) the fund looks very attractive in my eyes, although this is probably not the best time to buy it. I plan to increase my position with the money from the funds I decided to liquidate, hoping that the premium can be reduced.

RVT today has a purchase price located about 33 percent below the all-time high, marked on the eve of the Great Recession, which took its price to absolute lows. Since then, a long and convincing recovery to current values has begun, after the significant drop at the outbreak of the pandemic and the prodigious recovery the following year, which propelled it well above ($19.92) today's price. The trend appears to be upward, and its decent discount to NAV (-10.33%) makes it a fairly attractive fund in my eyes.

The Final Brushstroke

There are more things in heaven and earth, Horatio, than are dreamt of in your philosophy.” (Hamlet, 1.5.167-8)

Just as we cannot agree on the best movie, the best actor or the greatest jazz musician, even less do we share the criteria on the basis of which we make our investment choices. Each of us has a personal method of selecting securities for inclusion in our portfolios, based on a wide variety of parameters. There are dozens of them, all of which are certainly valid and proven, but as far as I am concerned, I am unable to appreciate the importance and validity of each of them. Let’s say that by necessity I have had to restrict my range of choices, thus reducing my color palette.

Over time, in fact, I decided to simplify my approach by adopting a method of selection based primarily on NAV performance, although today I have chosen to devote my attention only to historical price trends of my securities. A choice that, in this case, I would call “monochromatic,” consciously far from the variety of shades and the extraordinary coloristic effects of Pontormo and other great Mannerist painters, such as Rosso Fiorentino or the Sienese Domenico Beccafumi.

Personal note: These days the olive mills in my area are opening because the olive harvest to make new oil begins. Autumn, which the English Romantic poet John Keats called “season of mists and mellow fruitfulness,” is one of the most beautiful times of the year. Should you ever happen to visit Florence at the end of October, I suggest you to take an excursion into the hills or to the Chianti region and enter an olive mill. It is a wonderful experience amid colors, scents and flavors that tell a thousand-year old history.

data.title: Is EMB Really The Best ETF To Invest In Emerging Market Bonds? VWOB Might Be Better
data.description:
When considering the emerging bond market and seeking a passive fund solution to invest in it, the first choice is undoubtedly the iShares J.P. Morgan USD Emerging Markets Bond ETF (EMB). With an AUM of $15.53B, EMB is the largest passive fund designed to invest in the emerging bond market. The fund was established in 2009, and in my opinion, over the past 15 years, no truly competitive fund has emerged when it comes to sovereign and quasi-sovereign emerging bonds.

Therefore, I would like to start by analyzing the most well-known passive fund in the emerging bond market (EMB), and then examine its competitors in the sovereign or quasi-sovereign segment to see if there is indeed no better option than EMB.

My studies will lead to an interesting conclusion: the largest funds may not always be the best funds. In 2013, another passive fund was introduced, which initially seemed too risky, but its performance over the past 10 years suggests a different scenario: I'm speaking about Vanguard Emerging Markets Government Bond Index Fund ETF Shares (NASDAQ:VWOB).

What is the peer of EMB?

Let's start by analyzing EMB. First, I’d like to begin with a comparison analysis, asking myself: what is the true peer in the market?

These are the peers proposed by Seeking Alpha, but I would like to modify that list:

I'm adding the Vanguard Emerging Markets Government Bond (VWOB) and SPDR® Bloomberg Emerging Markets Local Bond ETF (EBND) while excluding the iShares J.P. Morgan EM Corporate Bond ETF (CEMB) and WisdomTree Emerging Markets Corporate Bond Fund ETF (EMCB). This is because I want to focus on funds primarily investing in sovereign bonds, which are issued by the governments of emerging market countries.

If you're interested in a comparison of corporate emerging market bonds, let me know in the comments section.

Now I'm going to look at the performance chart:

It’s easy to see that the best performer is the Global X Emerging Markets Bond ETF (EMBD), mainly because its holdings include a higher corporate component, whereas EMB invests in sovereign and quasi-sovereign entities. This is reflected in their differing credit quality exposure:

iShares J.P. Morgan USD Emerging Markets Bond ETF (EMB)

Global X Emerging Markets Bond ETF (EMBD)

So, I'm excluding EMBD from the comparison analysis, also because its AUM is significantly lower than other solutions in the market, not to mention that the fund is relatively new compared to its competitors.

For the same reason, I will also exclude the First Trust TCW Emerging Markets Debt ETF (EFIX) and the SPDR® DoubleLine® Emerging Markets Fixed Income ETF (EMTL) from my selection.

This is the final performance chart comparison, and we can see that, over the long term, the composition of EMB and VWOB outperformed EBND.

Let's do a quick comparison between the total returns:

So, despite EBND having the highest dividend yield (gains from interest, which I will refer to as dividend yield from now on) at around 5.63%, it had the lowest total return over the long term. Why? This is probably due to the inconsistency in its 'dividend yield' growth history.

At the same time, EBND invests in local currency-denominated bonds from emerging markets. This means that investors are exposed to fluctuations in emerging market currencies relative to the U.S. dollar.

For this reason, I’m excluding it from my selection.

EMB VS VWOB

And what about VWOB? The performance between EMB and VWOB is not significantly different. VWOB offers a higher dividend yield than its peers, around 5.67%, while EMB’s yield is 4.79%. Additionally, VWOB has a lower expense ratio than EMB: approximately 0.20% compared to 0.39% for EMB

The credit quality distribution is also quite different.

VWOB Vanguard Emerging Markets Government Bond Index Fund ETF Shares

It's important to remember that VWOB tracks the Bloomberg USD Emerging Markets Government RIC Capped Index, which focuses solely on sovereign debt from emerging market governments, unlike EMB. This point deserves particular attention, in my opinion. It means that VWOB includes in its portfolio sovereign bonds with lower ratings and may also invest in smaller, riskier emerging market countries, maintaining higher geographic diversification but, in reality, presenting more risk. This explains why the dividend yield is higher. Indeed, EMB has a more selective exposure to major emerging markets, potentially avoiding smaller and more volatile countries. Its focus on quasi-sovereign bonds provides additional stability.

Why in my opinion it's better choosing VWOB than EMB

In my portfolio, I chose to add VWOB instead of EMB for several reasons:

  • When considering a long-term investment, I’m well aware of the impact that the expense ratio has on final performance year over year, and VWOB's 0.20% expense ratio is the lowest in the market.
  • Even though EMB’s AUM is higher than VWOB’s, we’re talking about the two largest passive funds in the emerging market space, and there is absolutely no liquidity issue with either fund.
  • VWOB’s dividend yield is higher than EMB’s, and since its launch, VWOB has demonstrated good consistency in this regard

  • Even though VWOB holds bonds considered riskier than those in EMB’s portfolio (sovereign and quasi-sovereign bonds), the difference in standard deviation between the two funds is minimal (EMB standard deviation: 8.33; VWOB standard deviation: 8.39). However, over the last 10 years, the total return difference between these two funds is around 4%.

  • Lastly, a personal opinion: if I choose a passive fund that is truly focused on government emerging markets, the choice should go directly to VWOB. This is because the holdings of that ETF represent the entire emerging market debt, while EMB concentrates its investments in the sovereign and quasi-sovereign segments.

Risk

What’s the risk? Remember that VWOB has exposure to lower-rated sovereign bonds and may include smaller, riskier emerging market countries, while EMB invests in sovereign and quasi-sovereign bonds, meaning bonds issued by government-related entities in addition to national governments. Theoretically, during geopolitical issues, EMB should offer more stability, as its exposure should result in lower volatility. However, history has shown that this isn’t necessarily true. We’ve already discussed this, and the standard deviation doesn't demonstrate a significantly better risk coverage by EMB. For example, during periods of global market stress (such as the 2013 taper tantrum or the 2020 COVID-19 crisis), the drawdown of these two funds was quite similar.

Conclusion

Starting with the largest emerging market government bond ETF, EMB, we conclude with VWOB, the passive fund that, in my opinion, is the best on the market. VWOB offers a good combination of characteristics, including a lower expense ratio, higher dividend yield, better performance returns, and a consistent composition of holdings in the portfolio. In my opinion, it is a strong choice when looking to invest in this financial sector.

data.title: Third Point Q3 2024 Investor Letter
data.description:
Dear Investor:

During the third quarter, Third Point (OTCPK:TPNTF) returned 3.9% in the flagship Offshore Fund.

1 Through September 30, 2024. Please note there is a one-month lag in performance reflected for the CS HF Event-Driven Index

2 Annualized Return from inception (December 1996 for TP Offshore and quoted indices).

PLEASE SEE THE NEW SERIES RETURNS AT THE END OF THIS DOCUMENT.

The top five winners for the quarter were a private position in R2 Semiconductor, Pacific Gas and Electric Co. (PCG), Vistra Corp. (VST), KB Home (KBH), and Danaher Corp. (DHR)

The top five losers for the quarter, excluding hedges, were Bath & Body Works Inc. (BBWI), Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN), Advance Auto Parts Inc. (AAP), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG,GOOGL) and Microsoft Corp. (MSFT)

REVIEW AND OUTLOOK1

During the third quarter, Third Point Offshore generated gains of nearly 4%, bringing year- to-date returns to 13%, net of fees and expenses. Global equity markets continued their strong performance, but returns were driven by substantially more market breadth than over the previous year and a half. The “Magnificent Seven” trailed the broader market (albeit modestly) for the first time since Q4 2022. Rate sensitive stocks and cyclicals significantly outperformed as the market shifted its focus to the Fed’s long-awaited easing cycle. As we highlighted in our Second Quarter letter, our portfolio has a broad range of investment themes outside of large cap tech. These types of investments in industrials, utilities, materials, and other housing-sensitive stocks led the portfolio for Q3.

For most of the nearly thirty years I have run Third Point, the market has been inexorably climbing a wall of worry. At times, the worry turns to despair, most recently in the beginning of August, when the Nikkei (NKY:IND) inexplicably tanked roughly 20% in a few days and volatility in the US exploded to nearly 70 from 16, all while US markets dropped 6%. Many pundits saw this as a warning that the market had more room to drop and that, in the best case, stocks had become “un-investable” through the election. While we took our lumps for a few days, we stayed committed to our positions, took the view that the market rotation would continue, and increased our investments in event-driven and value-oriented stocks.

Considering political developments over the past few weeks, we believe that the likelihood of a Republican victory in the White House has increased, which would have a positive impact on certain sectors and the market overall. We believe the proposed “America First” policy’s tariffs will increase domestic manufacturing, infrastructure spending, and prices of certain materials and commodities. We also believe that a reduction in regulation generally and especially in the activist antitrust stance of the Biden-Harris administration will unleash productivity and a wave of corporate activity. Accordingly, we have increased certain positions that could benefit from such a scenario via both stock and option purchases and continue to shift our portfolio away from companies that will not. Whatever the outcome of the Presidential election, we have carefully studied the Senate races and believe that the Republicans will establish a majority, limiting the economic downside of a “Blue Sweep” which could theoretically usher in crushing taxes, stifling regulations, and a headwind to growth.

In the economy, we see no evidence of recession, slowing inflation, and a real interest rate that still needs to come down. We believe healthy consumer spending and active levels of individual investing should provide a liquidity backdrop to sustain market levels. We think this setup is a particularly good one for event-driven investing, particularly since most of our competitors in this area have retired or moved on. The potential for risk arbitrage transactions and corporate activity could usher in a golden age for the strategy. At this point, our gross exposures are low, we have modest nets, are well positioned in our current portfolio, and can deploy fresh capital as opportunities arise.

Equity Updates

DSV (OTCPK:DSDVF)

During the Third Quarter, we initiated a new position in the Danish freight forwarder DSV. DSV has come a long way from its origins as a Nordic road-hauler to become the world’s third largest freight forwarder, with a formidable track record of consolidating the fragmented global freight forwarding industry. We believe the company has an impressive culture that is systems-driven and returns-focused. DSV has generated an approximately 20% EPS CAGR over the past 10 years and is widely recognized as the best-in-class operator, with industry- leading growth and profit margins.

DSV emerged as the leading bidder in the auction of DB Schenker, a subsidiary of German state-owned Deutsche Bahn AG, and one of its largest competitors. DB Schenker is similar in size to DSV but only half as profitable. We believe the integration and synergy capture expected from this combination will follow a proven playbook and drive earnings accretion in excess of 30%. We have analyzed DSV’s many acquisitions and observed that they follow a pattern of swiftly migrating the target onto DSV’s IT system, culling low-margin business, and rightsizing the cost structure, resulting in the target’s margins reaching DSV’s best-in- class margins within two years.

The DB Schenker acquisition is taking place at an interesting time. Following a period of post-COVID earnings normalization and a CEO change, DSV’s stock was trading at an approximate 20% discount to both its lower-growth peers and its historical multiple. Following the deal, DSV will be the largest player in an industry in which scale brings tangible cost and network benefits. An example of this privileged competitive positioning is that DSV was selected as the exclusive logistics provider for Saudi Arabia’s NEOM project. We believe the joint venture between DSV and Saudi Arabia will provide end-to-end supply chain management, develop transport and logistic assets, and grow the company’s earnings power by about 15% by 2028.

We have spent time with Jens Lund, DSV’s long time COO/CFO who became CEO earlier this year and have found him to be laser-focused on creating shareholder value. Mr. Lund made a compelling case that rising complexity in global supply chains will benefit DSV, as it monetizes its unique network that guarantees capacity and on-time deliveries. In the freight forwarding industry, simple load, A-to-B transportation is barely profitable. The real money is made from value-added services such as customs clearance, load consolidation and intervention when problems occur, a core competency of DSV. We believe DSV can earn more than 100 DKK per share in 2027 and see significant upside for one of Europe’s best companies.

Cinemark (CNK)

Earlier this year we took a stake in Cinemark, the third largest movie theater chain in the U.S. We believe Cinemark is poised for underappreciated growth over the next few years as the supply of theatrical releases rebounds from pandemic- and strike-related headwinds. In addition, we believe Cinemark will gain share from undercapitalized competitors.

There is no shortage of skeptics about the move theater business. In 2020 the outlook for domestic cinemas looked bleak: the rapid rise of streaming, combined with behavior changes from the pandemic, cast doubt on whether people would ever go to theaters again. Regal Cinemas filed for bankruptcy. AMC became a meme stock.

Against this inauspicious backdrop, Cinemark has demonstrated resilient financial performance. Consider that in 2023, counterintuitively, Cinemark reported higher free cash flow than they did in the two years prior to the pandemic. Yet, Cinemark stock entered 2024 trading 70% below pre-pandemic levels (a mid-single digit multiple on trailing 12-month free cash flow), suggesting market participants feared free cash flow would drop precipitously and never recover. We disagree with this view and believe the multi-year outlook for Cinemark has never been more robust.

Despite the recent success of films such as “Inside Out 2”, 2024 industry revenues are expected to finish at approximately $8.5 billion, over 20% below pre-pandemic levels. While many in the market attribute this to changing consumer preferences, the data demonstrates theaters are a supply-driven industry (more films equal more foot traffic), and we believe the key driver of weak box office revenues has been a 20% decrease in widescreen theatrical releases since 2019. Importantly, we believe that this is driven by cyclical factors, namely labor stoppages from the pandemic and subsequently the strikes, rather than secular factors. Over the past three years, forays into exclusive streaming and day-and-date releases have proven too unprofitable, and the “event” aspect of a theatrical release has proven critical to securing top talent and creating franchise IP that can drive future earnings. As a result, all six major Hollywood studios have committed to ramp volume back up to pre-COVID levels, and even pureplay streamers like Amazon and Apple (AAPL) have begun releasing films exclusively in theaters. We expect supply to rebound next year and reach the pre-COVID level by 2026, which we expect to drive a full recovery in box office revenues as modestly lower per-film attendance is offset by price increases and growth in concession revenues. In our view, transient headwinds from the 2023 Hollywood strikes were masking this important secular shift in film supply, which gave us the opportunity to initiate the investment at a dislocated valuation.

Cinemark's earnings outperformance versus its peers through the pandemic has not been an accident; while AMC and Regal have been closing screens and underinvesting to preserve liquidity, Cinemark used what we see as a strong balance sheet and relentless focus on cost efficiencies to sustain maintenance capex in their theaters despite the challenged operating backdrop. As a result, the company has taken over 100 bps of market share, a trend we see as sustainable as peers continue to rationalize their footprint despite an improving market.

Given the significant recovery in box office, potential for continued share gain and high operating leverage of the business, we think Cinemark can generate over $4 of FCF/share in 2026, which is meaningfully higher than pre-pandemic levels and should grow in the following years. The company announced it will lay out its long-term capital allocation strategy in early 2025, including a re-introduction of a dividend, which should be supportive of a continued re-rating in the shares.

Credit Updates

Corporate Credit

Third Point’s corporate credit book generated a 3.5% gross return (3.4% net) during the quarter, contributing 50 basis points to performance. That puts year-to-date performance at +8.3% gross (8.2% net), in line with the high yield index. The summer proved anything but cruel for high yield, with the market returning 5.3% during the quarter, in line with the strong performance of the S&P 500. Spreads tightened marginally with most of the return driven by the decline in interest rates.

While some economic activity has been showing signs of slowing, the defensive composition of the current high yield market with a high mix of higher quality credit and short duration has let the rates tailwind overwhelm such concerns. The lowest quality sectors of the market have performed best, fueled by both soft/no landing expectations, as well as two positive events in the beleaguered telecom space. Telecom/cable have been poor performers year to date due to overhang from the growth of FWA (aka “wireless cable”) and increased fiber building, however the sector re-rated materially on two deals. First, Lumen Technologies (LUMN) announced that its Level 3 (LVLT) subsidiary was doing a fiber infrastructure build to support AI growth. Our aversion to secular decline (most of LUMN is melting copper infrastructure) kept us out of the situation but the AI fairy dust resulted in a massive rerating of LUMN debt and equity. These higher security prices in turn facilitated several moves to refinance portions of the capital structure and extend the runway.

Second, Verizon (VZ) announced a deal to acquire Frontier Communications (FYBR), a transaction which the fund benefited from by virtue of its investment in FYBR debt. This transaction, aimed at increasing’s VZ fiber footprint, has led to broad revaluation of fiber retail networks that we think is appropriate. While we continue to expect to see FWA rapidly erode non-upgraded cable and especially copper’s share of the low-end broadband market, the VZ deal underscores the value of the higher end footprint.

Much has been written about “creditor on creditor violence” – liability management deals where issuers are able to reduce their cost of capital or extend liquidity runways by offering a subset of creditors a chance to move up the capital stack at the expense of their brethren. These are frequently less than zero sum deals for creditors and primarily benefit financial sponsors, and a large number end up restructuring anyway after paying enormous fees to lawyers and advisors. As a result, we are seeing a rising number of creditor “co-op agreements”, which serve to prevent sponsors from manipulating creditors. While we have generally been very careful to position ourselves on the winning side of these skirmishes, we are pleased to see the rise of these co-op agreements. Co-ops can make investing in highly stressed situations more attractive because you can trust that a senior obligation does not get leapfrogged by a junior obligation. Additionally, it seems likely that these co-ops will accelerate the pace of restructurings, since sponsors will have limited options to buy time to avoid equity write-offs.

While the high yield market has rallied, we have continued to find opportunity in a few areas. We have bought into several credits that have gone through liability management deals. These businesses were improving, and recapitalization was comprehensive enough to “fix” the balance sheet. We are also finding value in several loan-only structures that have lagged the rally in the high yield market.

Structured Credit

The Structured Credit portfolio contributed 20 basis points in the quarter, driven by Treasuries and credit spreads rallying. While the Treasury market has likely overestimated the magnitude of potential Fed rate cuts for this year, we took advantage of that market window and exercised our call rights on eight reperforming mortgage deals this quarter. We priced a new mortgage securitization in August with AAA’s pricing inside of 5%, closer to investment grade yields we saw in 2019 and early 2020. As insurance companies and private credits funds actively look for investment grade risk, we have been able to access, in our view, attractive cost of funds across structured credit loans. Given the decline in new mortgage originations and newly issued mortgage-backed securities, we have seen an improvement in the technical backdrop for existing securities and loans. We believe this dynamic gives us an advantage as we continue to sell and optimize our existing mortgage portfolio.

On the ABS front, yields have continued to compress across all asset classes. Spreads have remained largely unchanged on our rental car ABS portfolio, which we bought earlier this year at double digit yields. This has been a positive trade for the portfolio as we realize significant carry each month. We have been monetizing our ABS positions into this credit spread tightening and are spending more time on CLOs and CMBS as credit events start to play out.

As we face geopolitical uncertainty and a volatile interest rate environment, we anticipate interesting investment opportunities in the Fourth Quarter as investors look to protect a solid 2024 performance.

Private Position Update: R2 Semiconductor

In March, we disclosed that we were supporting R2 Semiconductor, a private company in our Third Point ventures portfolio that we invested in over 15 years earlier, as it sought to enforce its patented technology against Intel. The technology, developed by R2’s Founder David Fisher, relates to integrated voltage regulation, which plays an essential part in reducing power consumption by microchips while maintaining product reliability.

At the end of August, Intel announced that its dispute with R2 had been fully settled in all jurisdictions. The terms are confidential, but we are pleased with the outcome, which resulted in a significant gain in the position for the quarter.

Business Updates

Matthew Ressler joined Third Point’s private credit team in Q3. Prior to joining Third Point, Mr. Ressler spent four years at Apollo Global Management as an investor in the Private Equity Group, with a focus in the technology, industrials and consumer sectors. Mr. Ressler also previously worked at Moelis & Company as an Associate in the firm’s Investment Banking division after beginning his career at JPMorgan Chase. Mr. Ressler holds an MBA from Harvard Business School and a B.A from Dartmouth College.

Ted Smith-Windsor joined Third Point in Q3, focusing on credit investment opportunities. Prior to joining Third Point, Mr. Smith-Windsor worked at Silver Point Capital where he focused on investments in distressed credit and special situations. He began his career at CPPIB where he focused on investments in private equity and credit. Mr. Smith-Windsor is a graduate of the University of Toronto, where he earned a B. Comm. in Finance and Economics.

Maureen Hart joined Third Point in Q3 as Head of Consultant Relations. Prior to joining Third Point, Ms. Hart was a Partner at Albourne America. Over her 12 years at Albourne, she oversaw many of the firm’s North American clients, managed a global 50-person team, and led the firm’s cross-selling initiative. Ms. Hart began her career in Investor Relations at FrontPoint Partners, covering equity long/short funds. She graduated from the University of Connecticut with a B.A. in English.

Thomas Anglin joined Third Point in Q3 as Head of Marketing and Business Development for the Asia-Pacific region. Prior to joining Third Point, Mr. Anglin was a Managing Director at Goldman Sachs in Hong Kong where he oversaw coverage of hedge fund managers in Asia and was responsible for Goldman Sachs' Australian Prime Brokerage business. Previously, he held senior leadership positions at Goldman Sachs in New York, UBS Investment Bank in New York and Sydney, and was an equities and commodities trader at Ospraie Management. Mr. Anglin started his career in equity derivatives sales and trading at Macquarie Bank in Australia and moved to New York with Macquarie in 2000. He received a B.Com from Monash University in Melbourne and is a CFA charterholder.

Sincerely,

Daniel S. Loeb CEO

data.title: ASML: Timing Matters - Here's Why I'm Waiting
data.description:
ASML Holding (NASDAQ:ASML) crashed 16% on Tuesday and another 5% on Wednesday, marking one of its steepest declines in 20 years, after the company accidentally announced its Q3 2024 earnings a day earlier than previously anticipated. Even worse, the earnings report showed a significant miss on net order bookings and a revision of its previous 2025 guidance, highlighting continued weakness in the equipment-making industry with a recovery now pushed likely beyond the H1 2025.

The report sent shockwaves through the industry, sending other majors such as Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) down 4.7% and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) down 2.6% for the day.

Previous Coverage

Just three weeks ago, I published an article on ASML's stock, labeled "Market Pullback Offers Opportunity, Valued At 25x FY25 Earnings," sharing my bullish view on the lithography machine maker amidst the AI chip production frenzy. I previously expected the US/Dutch government restrictions not to have a material impact on 2025 ASML's earnings as the demand would be compensated by newly built fabs in Europe and the US, but continuous delays are delaying the revenue recognition, while the traditional end markets are recovering slower-than-expected due to cautious consumers, reducing the need for more production capacity buildup in the near-term.

Despite the near-term challenges, particularly in the logic of the slower recovery of end markets such as mobile and PC, the AI story is expected to continue with potential upside in 2025, contrary to some analysts calling this "the end of the AI investment narrative."

The weak guidance is troubling and highlights the larger underlying issues of sufficient capacity in the chip-making business. Yet, ASML's fundamentals remain strong, and investors willing to add shares on the weakness might be well-rewarded once the dust settles. However, reversal may take time, well into mid-2025, to play out, and buying the shares at today's price of €630/share might be "dead money" for a few months. Hence, I am not rushing to buy more shares right away.

The Earnings & Outlook

ASML's early Q3 earnings release, due to a "technical error," left many investors, myself included, with a sour taste. The early earnings release seems like a "rookie mistake" and likely further contributed to the decline in the stock price, given that both order intake and 2025 were major disappointments.

However, the actual Q3 earnings were solid, driven by better-than-expected DUV system sales and higher installed base management fees. Chinese customers frontloaded their orders ahead of anticipated new export restrictions expected to set in from 2025. Overall, ASML reported €7.5B in Net Sales for Q3, surpassing previous analysts' expectations. The net system sales remained well-balanced, with 64% in memory and 36% in logic.

The gross margin remained strong at 50.8%, within ASML's management guidance, thanks to R&D costs being in check and coming below expectations at €1.06B while SG&A of €297M aligned with the guidance. The EPS came at €5.28/share.

ASML closed Q3 with a free cash flow of €534M, a modest improvement from the previous quarter. However, free cash flow remains under pressure due to lower order intake, resulting in fewer down payments and higher inventory levels due to higher inventory build-up of both high-NA and low-NA systems due to longer lead times in the production cycle and preparation for future ramp-up.

Nonetheless, investors' primary concern is ASML's Q3 net booking, which came at just €2.6B, falling short of the estimated €5.6B. This shortfall is a direct result of the slower-than-anticipated recovery of the broader semis market (beyond AI). Yet, it's vital to understand that ASML's backlog is still healthy at €36B, equating to roughly 5.5 quarters of sales if we assume an average of €6.5B worth of backlog is fulfilled each quarter.

Management has shared their viewpoint, acknowledging the demand for AI-chip-making equipment remains strong; the non-AI segments are expected to witness a more gradual recovery than previously anticipated. That means 2024 will be a "transitory year," as previously flagged, and H1 2025 as well, with an expected recovery in H2 2025 and into 2026.

The unfavorable headwind is also reflected in ASML's reduced sales forecast for 2025, now projected to land between €30-35B, compared to the previous projection of as high as €40B, announced back in 2022 during Investor's Day. For Q4, ASML's management is still expected to deliver around €9B in sales (another strong quarter helped by strong demand from China), with 2024 full-year sales now estimated to land around €28B (2% YoY growth compared to 2023, confirming the transitory year narrative). The cautious 2025 view will likely result in 16% sales growth at a midpoint of €32.5B—not a tragedy if you ask me, but enough to spook investors with the guidance coming at the lower end than consensus.

ASML is planning another Investor's Day on November 14, 2024, where management is expected to share their long-term guidance through 2030. This will likely be another key event that sheds light on the momentum of the semis recovery. If you are undecided whether you want to add shares of ASML at today's price, I recommend waiting until this event to gain more clarity, as the stock may remain relatively volatile.

ASML's reduced exposure to the Chinese market will undoubtedly pose some level of headwind in 2025, mainly as Europe and the US are slow in building up new foundries as part of the project to onshore critical infrastructure production, pushing revenue recognition further into H2 2025 and 2026.

As a result of this negative development, analysts have reduced their 2025 EPS growth from the previous 59% growth down to 25% while raising expectations for 2026 as demand remains strong—however, the timing weighs on ASML's near-term growth.

Valuation & Forward Growth

I discussed valuation in great length in my last coverage, so I will provide here just a short update on where the stock is now trading, following the fall from €797 to €629/share following the earnings release.

ASML's shares have traded at an average valuation of around 31.7x in the last 15 years, with an annualized EPS growth of 14% over the same duration.

Following the crash, ASML's shares are now priced at 32.62x its Blended P/E, the earnings of the last two quarters and the next two quarters.

As per FactSet, analysts are now expecting the following EPS growth (for ADR shares):

  • FY2024: EPS of $20.75E, -4% YoY growth
  • FY2025: EPS of $26.00E, 25% YoY growth
  • FY2026: EPS of $32.71E, 26% YoY growth

That's a contraction from the previously expected $32.69 in 2025 and $37.37 in 2026.

I retain my "Strong Buy" rating on ASML's shares, as the expected ROR of around 22% annually over the next 2–3 years is very attractive. However, as I said previously, the stock needs some time to consolidate, and it might be "dead money" for a few months, with some volatility expected around the next Investor's Day.

Personally, I am in no rush to add more shares of ASML as the growth has been pushed beyond 2025, but if the stock drops below €600/share, I will add more shares to dilute the cost basis.

Investor's Takeaway

ASML delivered strong Q3 earnings, but disappointing net booking and 2025 guidance at the lower end of management's previous expectations have led to major disappointment among investors and a sell-off. The early release of the earnings may have exacerbated the pullback even further.

Despite the lower recovery in the non-AI chipmaking segment, demand for AI-related chips remains strong and will likely serve as a tailwind over the following years. Due to normalizing demand and additional export curbs, ASML's dependency on China is expected to drop significantly from 49% this year to 20% next year.

From a fundamental standpoint, ASML's business remains strong but faces adverse macroeconomic headwinds, with the broader semis recovery now pushed into H2 2025 and 2026. In my view, investors do not need to rush to buy ASML's stock, but they should better let the dust settle, potentially waiting for Investor's Day in November, as the stock may witness more volatility.

data.title: After A Month Of Buying, Institutional Investors Back To Selling Stocks
data.description:
The surprising shift by institutional investors to become net buyers of stocks appears to have lasted just one month, as they returned to their positions as the biggest sellers in the equity market in September.

Institutions sold a net $29.93 billion in equities throughout September, their highest level of selling since May and a reversal from August when the group bought nearly $6.82 billion in stocks, according to the latest data from S&P Global Market Intelligence.

Institutions sold a net $241.43 billion from the end of September 2023 to the end of September 2024, even as the S&P 500 rallied more than 34% over that time, the data shows.

"Institutions are adopting a more defensive position as we enter the final quarter of 2024," said Thomas McNamara, a director for Issuer Solutions with S&P Global Market Intelligence. "Several factors are driving this approach, including the upcoming election, ongoing conflicts in the Middle East, Federal Reserve policy, and uncertainty about the presidential outcome."

In spite of these lingering concerns, however, institutions boosted their positions in consumer discretionary sector stocks by 0.4% in September, the top buy for the group for the month. The move into consumer discretionary suggests that institutional investors may have some optimism about the path of the economy as the Fed begins to ease monetary policy through lower interest rates, McNamara said.

Retail investors, meanwhile, reduced their exposure to consumer discretionary stocks by 1.3% in September, the sector that saw the most selling for the group throughout the month.

"Retail investors sought to capitalize on short-term gains, with the sector emerging as the top performer following the Fed's rate cut," McNamara said. "In contrast, institutions, with a longer-term perspective, viewed September as the potential onset of sustained stock price appreciation in the sector, driven by the expectation of continued interest rate reductions."

Overall, capital flows in September seemed to reflect more long running trends. Retail investors sold off $9.38 billion in September, $136.37 billion over the past year; index and exchange-traded funds bought $12.71 billion, $195.93 billion over the course of the year; and hedge funds bought $10.74 billion after buying $22.44 billion in August, continuing to switch from buying and selling each month. Hedge funds have bought a net $8.95 billion since the end of September 2023.

Hedge funds have been employing two divergent strategies recently.

The first strategy, McNamara said, is seizing on trading volatility, buying during stock market dips and selling at potential peaks. This strategy pays little regard to the longer-term direction of the market. The second strategy has hedge funds allocate capital towards more passive investment in order to capture broader market gains as momentum increases in the overall economy.

data.title: Greenlight Capital Q3 2024 Letter
data.description:
Dear Partner:

The Greenlight Capital funds (the “Partnerships”) returned 1.1%1 in the third quarter of 2024, net of fees and expenses, compared to 5.9% for the S&P 500 index. (SP500,SPX)

There was a lot going on in the world this quarter. Nevertheless, we had a very quiet period, partly because we remained conservatively positioned with very low exposure to equity beta. As we discussed at our Partner Dinner in January, the economy remains relatively strong, with nominal annualized growth now running at 5-6%. On the other hand, however, the market isn’t just making all-time highs. It is, by many measures, the most expensive stock market that we have seen since the founding of Greenlight.

Warren Buffett is, of course, the most successful investor of his generation, and maybe of all time. While Mr. Buffett routinely points out that it is impossible to time the market, we can’t help but observe that he has been one of the best market timers we have ever seen. When the market got too frothy in the late 1960s, he closed his fund. Towards the market bottom in the early 1970s, he re-emerged as a stock picker and then prior to the 1987 crash, he sold everything except a couple of illiquid holdings. Later, he sidestepped the various crises in corporate credit and was well-positioned to capitalize on the 2008 global financial crisis. One could argue that sitting out bear markets has been the underappreciated reason for his outstanding long-term returns. It is therefore noteworthy to observe that Mr. Buffett is again selling large swaths of his stock portfolio and building enormous cash reserves.

Our sense is that Mr. Buffett’s portfolio adjustments are not a prediction that the market will fall next week, next month, or even next quarter. Rather, these stock sales more likely express a long-term view that right now is not a great time to have a lot of equity exposure, and that the opportunity set is expected to be better at some point in the not-so-distant future. We will avoid calling this market a bubble, and simply observe that the dividend yield is low and the P/E ratio is elevated despite corporate earnings being cyclically high, if not top-of-cycle.

This time, it is not just a handful of high-profile technology companies with nosebleed valuations. In fact, it is easy to find mature, industrial businesses with cyclical exposure and growth prospects that aren’t materially better than the current nominal GDP growth rate that currently trade for 30-50x earnings. Obviously, the market appreciation thus far in 2024 has exceeded the growth rate of revenues and earnings. We think Paul Tudor Jones is right when he says that managing the last third of a great bull or bear market move is often the toughest.

However, while we are conservatively positioned with respect to net exposure, we aren’t outright bearish. We are likely to continue to underperform a rising market, as we have all year, but we don’t wish to position ourselves to lose money should the market continue to rise.

Notably, almost all of our return this year has been alpha. While being up 9% year-to-date when the S&P 500 index is up more than 20% over the same period doesn’t feel great, on a beta- adjusted basis we believe we are doing fine. If you happen to be invested in our gold share class, it’s better than fine.

During the quarter, both our longs and shorts rose marginally less than the market. The macro portfolio had another strong quarter, driven by the sharp rise in the price of gold. We also lost money on equity index hedges.

In addition to gold, Green Brick Partners (GRBK) was the other significant winner during the quarter. GRBK reported quarterly earnings of $2.32 per share, which beat analyst estimates of $1.77 per share. The company continues to lead the industry in gross margins and has distinguished itself from its peers, who are increasingly focused on appearing to be “land light.” GRBK believes that being land light is very expensive at the gross margin level. Rather than pay high rates to third-party off-balance sheet land bankers, GRBK owns most of its land and lots while maintaining a very low-leveraged investment-grade balance sheet. As a result, it earns all of the development profits in addition to the homebuilding profits. Despite being “land heavy,” the result is a return on equity that is near the top of the industry. Even more exciting, GRBK has had a very successful period of land acquisition, and as a result the prospects for it to continue delivering industry-leading margins remain promising. This quarter, GRBK shares advanced from $57.24 to $83.52.

On the losing side, ODP Corporation (ODP) reported a second consecutive disappointing quarter. Sales and profits came in well below expectations and the shares reacted by falling from $39.27 to $29.75.

The short book had a material loser in a housing-related short. We believe this company is structurally unprofitable and faces looming debt maturities that could be challenging to refinance at a reasonable interest rate. Nonetheless, it was a strong period for all things housing- related as the sector celebrated the move lower in yields.

While we don’t have any new positions to reveal this quarter, we have identified three new longs that we will likely discuss in the near future. However, as we are still in the accumulation phase, it is premature to describe them now.

We exited a few positions during the quarter, including:

NeuBase Therapeutics (NBSE): We described this nascent company in our Q4 2020 letter. Ultimately, NBSE ran out of money and failed. We do like to have a few small lottery tickets in the portfolio, but this one didn’t hit. Our NBSE investment was less than 1% at cost, which we lost most of over our five-year holding period.

NatWest Group (United Kingdom: NWG): We sold after achieving a 20% IRR over almost two years. The thesis played out and we no longer judged the shares to be materially undervalued.

Ryanair (Ireland: RYAAY): We exited with a modest profit after three and a half years. For some time, the post-COVID environment was favorable, allowing the company to raise prices and improve its earnings. Alas, that turned. Recently, pricing has reversed and earnings have been disappointing. As a result, we wound up surrendering what had been some decent unrealized gains.

At the end of the quarter, we said goodbye to David Tepperman who decided to pursue new adventures. David was with us for over 15 years, and we want to thank him for the long period of contribution.

On September 23, Toby and Ruth Haselberger welcomed the newest addition to the Greenlight family with the birth of Gia Isabella. Big sister Coco was thrilled to meet her baby sister “Bella.” Congratulations to the Haselbergers!

Please save the date for our 29 th Annual Partner Dinner, which will be held on January 21, 2025 in New York.

At quarter-end, the largest disclosed long positions in the Partnerships were Brighthouse Financial (BHF), CONSOL Energy (CEIX), Green Brick Partners (GRBK), HP and Solvay (OTCPK:SVYSF). The Partnerships had an average exposure of 97% long and 64% short.

“You are allowed to be both a masterpiece and a work in progress, simultaneously.” – Sophia Bush

Best Regards,

Greenlight Capital
