data.title: Interpublic Group: May Not Even Have The Growth Edge Anymore
data.description:
Interpublic Group of Companies (NYSE:IPG), or IPG for short, put in a decent performance as of the last earnings some months ago with modest topline performance and a bit of operating profit growth. This is in line with our previous coverage where we also noted solid cost performance despite pretty limited topline growth action, which is consistent with the current macro environment which pressures ad spend, but also supports the company's ambitious guidance for meaningful EBITA margin improvement for the FY. They confirmed the growth downgrade in the Q2 due to a reduction in some of the healthcare markets with an important client scaling back spend in March, and this had compromised a better growth outlook on the back of a recovery in tech which has been a weaker market so far which could have been the clincher for overcoming toughening comps in H2. The company's focus on the North American market had at least kept growth in the cards, particularly as a lagging tech and telco market looks to recover towards the end of the year. Europe has also been an idiosyncratically strong performer overall for IPG as of Q2.

However, while they are a solid pick operationally, and at least avoid excessive and potentially troublesome Chinese exposure, they lost some more business in September to their competitors. This means growth guidance should be further compromised and that should become evident in the Q4, and might affect guidance comments in the coming Q3 earnings. Even more importantly, they are pricier than peer WPP (WPP) in terms of forward PE, and we think that in a very mature and competitive industry like advertising, the value of growth opportunities are more marginal in the valuation picture and the help of a discounted multiple is more important in gunning for a return. With the combination of the recent business losses that may cloud announcements and the more premium multiple, we prefer other picks within the sector over IPG.

Q2 earnings and looking to upcoming Q3

The biggest IPG market is the US at around 66% of net revenues in the Q2. The US consumer remained strong even after the close of books, but there was still some general pressure in terms of ad spending by customers with reticence around the US market by US corporates. Growth was 1.3% organically YoY for the US which was in line with peers and reflects a pretty solid macro backdrop in the US with the consumer there still strong, but it was in the end the international markets that saw more powerful growth at 2.6% organically.

This international performance was not really consistent with peers, where Europe was generally a bit shaky, and was even trending down in the UK and the Western parts of Europe for WPP.

IPG saw acceleration and strong performance, owing apparently to a couple of large accounts that have an idiosyncratic effect on the business. The UK grew 3.4% organically where PR saw solid growth, and continental Europe grew a very solid 6.3% marking considerable share wins over more focused European players like indeed WPP where these markets in the same quarter either saw more limited growth or outright shrank.

The delta between the European and UK performance and overall ex-US performance comes from APAC which was down a clear 5% or so, consistent with the drag from macro-pressured geographies like China which are likely to remain a problem. In general, it should be noted that all these businesses, focused on the spending wallet for advertising at corporates, is pretty sensitive to macro and not recession resistant, as advertising is in itself both clearly cyclical and also discretionary. It's often basically at the click of a button that corporates can dial back advertising spend. That's why we are not crazy about APAC exposure right now.

Guided growth was still expected to be relatively flat in terms of the topline despite solid growth so far in the year as of the Q2 call. This is consistent with the expectations of IPG peers as well, even those with less favourable markets in the mix, namely WPP which has a much larger Chinese and APAC business (approx. 25% of sales compared to 7% for IPG) that is expected to be a challenging market into the end of the year and beyond due to the property deleveraging situation. However, the similar growth guidance is for different reasons. There was an idiosyncratic hit that tapered growth earlier on in the year for IPG.

On our last call with you, we noted that due to a decision by a major and ongoing health care client in late March related to their global consumer advertising work, the high end of the 1% to 2% growth range, that we had targeted as we entered the year would not be achievable... [1% guidance in effect].

Philippe Krakowsky, CEO of IPG Q2 2024 Call

There was also a call-out in the last quarter that the macro environment is looking a bit tougher going forward. IPG, as opposed to WPP, also has tougher comps going into the H2, with H2 2023 having been pretty solid at IPG with considerable sequential topline development ahead of WPP's in percentage growth. From March to December 2023, WPP had less than 10% sequential improvement, while IPG had around 15%.

We expect that tech in particular, which has been a weak area across advertising so far this year, should see a bit of a rebound towards the end of the year and support growth where some was idiosyncratically lost in an important health account at IPG.

Going the other way, we saw decreases in the financial services, tech and telecom and auto and transportation sectors.

Ellen Johnson, CFO of IPG Q2 2024 Call

And then on the tech sector, we talked about the stabilization that we have seen in Q2. And we would expect to see a movement into growth in the second half.

Joanne Wilson, CFO of WPP Q2 2024 Call

The same rebound effect was mentioned by peers looking forward and should be lifting them as well as we come around to the Q3.

WPP was looking at something like 0-1% in guided growth for the FY as of their Q2 call. IPG was guiding only slightly ahead of that, just north of 1%, on the basis of a healthcare account that has shrunk on a go forward basis but also notably tougher comps. IPG's end markets geographically are healthier than WPP's, which is more levered to China which is having protracted macroeconomic problems. However, with the announcement of lost business from Amazon (AMZN) in September, the growth guidance should deteriorate further, and is likely now slightly in negative territory which could even put it behind peers like WPP despite WPP's more challenged APAC exposures.

In terms of guidance, WPP is looking at around 5-6% operating profit improvement coming in from just the cost savings plan in 2024. IPG is guiding a 16.6% EBITA margin for the FY, with the Q2 running at 14.6% meaning an expectation of significant improvement. In addition to concerns around the topline guidance, profit guidance could also be impacted, since 180 bps in margin improvement YoY in the Q2 came from operating leverage on payroll. Those positive effects could go into partial reverse if growth indeed ends up negative due to the recently lost business in September.

Bottom Line

FGS Global saw a partial sale to KKR (KKR) which has helped us at least with a precedent for valuing PR businesses at least, and brought some absolute appeal due to low multiples on the residual businesses.

We didn't see specific disclosure as far as PR revenues at IPG, which gets more clearly broken out at WPP, but IPG's specialised communications segment at around 15% of revenues for IPG does contain the PR business. The PR businesses at WPP are valued at around 20x EV/EBITDA multiples based on the FGS precedent. Assuming the same mix of PR revenues at IPG as at WPP, its own non-PR businesses could be valued at around 6.4x. WPP's non-PR businesses are at around 5.2x. Note that effective tax rates between the two companies are similar enough to not be able to explain the extent of the difference in those multiples. Also, annualising PEs at IPG and assuming a normal tax rate you get something like a 15-16x forward PE compared to WPP at around 12-13x. The difference is similar in multiple when using Seeking Alpha's consensus forecast-based forward PEs, around 11-12x for IPG versus 8-9x for WPP (from different sources and forecast pools it's around 12.2x IPG to 11.4x for WPP on a forward PE basis).

IPG benefits in that WPP has more undesirable APAC exposure, but with ultimately pretty similar growth guidance as of the Q2, and with WPP and Omnicom (OMC) outcompeting IPG for some important business from Amazon, WPP's outlook is improving at this point with the IPG outlook deteriorating. With the announcement of this lost business being recent, we are not terribly excited about IPG's upcoming earnings next week as the modest growth guidance will probably be further tempered and operating leverage benefits that drove margin performance in Q2 potentially reversing.

We had strong leverage on base payroll which helped drive 180 basis points of operating leverage on salaries and related expenses compared to a year ago...

Philippe Krakowsky, CEO of IPG

We have concerns around China and APAC at WPP, where macro pressures are more protracted, but we'd rather seek refuge within the sector in a stock with more margin of safety thanks to a discounted multiple. So we eschew IPG.
